data.title: German Inflation Slows; Unemployment Falls
data.description:
BRUSSELS (dpa-AFX) - Germany's inflation slowed in May but core price pressures accelerated on higher service costs, and unemployment declined unexpectedly in April after a notable increase, official data showed Friday.

Reflecting the slowdown in energy price growth, consumer price inflation weakened to 2.6 percent in May, preliminary estimate from Destatis revealed. Inflation rate was forecast to stay steady at 2.9 percent, which was the highest rate since December 2023.

Likewise, EU harmonized inflation slowed to 2.7 percent from 2.9 percent a month ago. The harmonized inflation was also expected to remain unchanged at 2.9 percent.

However, core inflation that excludes prices of food and energy accelerated to 2.5 percent from 2.3 percent in the previous month.

Services inflation climbed to 3.1 percent from 2.8 percent, while goods prices grew at a slower pace of 2.2 percent compared to 2.9 percent a month ago.

Energy prices logged an annual growth of 6.6 percent compared to the 10.1 percent rise in April. Food price inflation eased to 0.4 percent from 1.2 percent.

The decline in headline inflation was due to the government's so-called tax rebate, ING economist Carsten Brzeski said.

'...today's inflation numbers should not be read as a sign that the inflation wave is already over before it actually started but rather as a confirmation that this is a relatively mild inflation wave,' Brzeski said.

The economist expects inflation to crawl up in the coming months, probably reaching 4 percent by late summer.

Elsewhere, the Federal Employment Agency reported that the German jobless rate dropped to 6.3 percent in May from 6.4 percent in April. The rate was seen unchanged at 6.3 percent.

The number of unemployed declined unexpectedly by 12,000 in May, partly reversing a 19,000 increase in April. Economists had forecast a rise of 11,000.

The decline was mainly due to the counter effect following the weak April figures, the agency said.

Despite a decline in unemployment, the spring upturn has not really gained momentum this year, Federal Employment Agency Chairwoman Andrea Nahles said.

The labor force survey data from Destatis showed that the unemployment rate remained stable at an adjusted 3.8 percent in April. The number of unemployed decreased 13,000 from the previous month to 1.67 million.

On an unadjusted basis, the jobless rate rose to 3.9 percent in April from 3.8 percent in the previous year.

Copyright(c) 2026 RTTNews.com. All Rights Reserved

Copyright RTT News/dpa-AFX

data.title: ONDS’ Stock Draws Investor Buzz After Q2 Order Update  – Retail Calls It An Order-Story The 'Market Can't Ignore For Long'
data.description:
Key points:
  • CEO Eric Brock stated that Ondas is well-positioned to meet evolving requirements through expanding its technology portfolio, operational capabilities, and growing global customer relationships.
  • The company plans to continue advancing its growth strategy by increasing production capacity and integrating acquired technology.
  • Ondas reported a pro forma backlog of $457 million at the end of the first quarter.

Shares of Ondas Inc. (ONDS) drew significant investor attention on Friday after the company announced it secured more than $30 million in new orders during May, bringing second-quarter-to-date orders to over $110 million across its defense, security, and autonomous technology platform.

The May momentum shows accelerating customer demand for air defense, counter-UAS, loitering munitions, ISR systems, and robotic ground vehicles.

New Orders In Second Quarter So Far

Ondas reported a pro forma backlog of $457 million at the end of the first quarter. The company said the orders are helping to build its growing backlog and span its defense and security platforms, including air defense, counter-drone systems, loitering munitions, surveillance and reconnaissance, unmanned ground vehicles, robotic defense systems, and mission-critical security tech.

"Securing over $30 million in new orders during May, and over $110 million in orders quarter-to-date, reflects Ondas' continued execution," said Eric Brock, Chairman and Chief Executive Officer at Ondas.

“We believe this momentum is strengthening our visibility into future growth opportunities across defense, homeland security, and critical infrastructure markets,” Brock added.

He stated that the government and critical infrastructure operators are increasingly seeking proven partners capable of delivering integrated autonomous systems, counter-drone tech, and related mission solutions at scale.

ONDS Well Positioned To Meet Demands

Brock highlighted that Ondas is well-positioned to support these evolving requirements through its expanding technology portfolio, operational capabilities, and growing global customer relationships.

The company plans to continue advancing its growth strategy by increasing production capacity, integrating acquired technologies, deepening customer relationships, and pursuing larger defense and security contracts across the U.S., Europe, the Middle East, and other allied markets.

What Retails Thinks About ONDS

The retail sentiment surrounding the stock has remained ‘extremely bullish’ while message volumes improved to ‘extremely high’ from ‘high’ over the past 24 hours. Retail chatter on ONDS stock rose more than 410% over the past week.

One user on Stocktwits said Ondas is starting to look like one of those order-flow stories the market can't ignore.

https://stocktwits.com/capitalthinktank/message/654759747

Another user said defense names are quietly setting up again, and this sector looks far from done.

https://stocktwits.com/ChipDistribution7/message/654771834

Ondas shares have gained over 6% year-to-date.

data.title: W.Africa Crude-IOC snaps up WAF, Nigerian programmes emerge
data.description:
West African crude differentials in the spot market were steady on Friday, while India's IOC picked up Angolan and Nigerian crude in a recent buy tender.

  • India's IOC purchased cargoes of Angolan Nemba and Kissanje from Shell and BP respectively, for delivery to Paradip. It also bought Nigerian Usan from ExxonMobil for delivery to Vadinar.

  • The West African cargoes traded at around dated Brent plus $4, trade sources said.

  • Exports of Nigerian Bonny Light, Bonga, Forcados and Qua Iboe are scheduled at about 847,000 barrels per day in July, preliminary loading programmes seen by Reuters show.

  • At that level, loadings will be about 2.4% higher than the 827,000 bpd scheduled to load for those grades in June.

  • A small number of May West African cargoes are loaded on water and not yet heading to destinations, a trader said on Friday.

  • West African differentials have slid in recent weeks from record highs amid stiff competition from U.S. and Americas crudes, and a drop-off in Asian demand.

  • Trades for July loadings were slow to emerge, traders said.

data.title: CFTC opens door for crypto perpetual future contracts in US as Coinbase, Kalshi move forward
data.description:
The Commodity Futures Trading Commission opened the doors for perpetual futures contracts, giving way for Kalshi and Coinbase to offer those products in the United States.

The move is significant since most of that activity had been offshore and not in the U.S. Perpetuals, or perps, are a type of futures contract that don't have an expiration date and allow people to bet on the price movement of assets without owning them directly. They've become increasingly popular in crypto derivatives trading.5

"This morning, the @CFTC took historic action to permit the listing of a true bitcoin perpetual contract by a CFTC-registered exchange, charting a path for one of the most liquid segments of the crypto asset markets to exist within the US regulatory framework," said CFTC Chair Michael Selig in a post on X.

On Friday, the CFTC's Division of Clearing and Risk, Division of Market Oversight and Market Participation Division issued that staff advisory, and it is not a formal rulemaking that offers more permanency. In the advisory, CFTC staff said they noticed more interest in 24/7 trading, driven in part by blockchain technology and decentralized infrastructure.

"Therefore, Commission staff believes that an advisory, outlining the potential risks associated with 24/7 trading, clearing, and settlement, and the ways in which these risks are addressed by current Commission regulations, may help promote continued market robustness, along with responsible innovation and fair competition among market participants," the staff said in the advisory.

The CFTC greenlit KalshiEX, LLC to list a perpetual contract that is tied to the price of bitcoin, the BTCPERP Contract. Also on Friday, the CFTC issued a no-action stance for Coinbase Financial Markets, Inc., for its plans to offer digital commodity derivatives products.

"Big day for our US-based traders, and for Coinbase," said Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong in a post on X. "Until now, US users have been locked out of ~80% of global crypto markets (perpetual futures and options). But not anymore!"

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Europe's Gas Prices Seen Staying Elevated Amid LNG Supply Disruptions — Market Talk
data.description: European gas prices are expected to remain well above pre-war levels through the end of the year due to expected delays in replenishing the region's gas storage sites amid continued disruption to LNG flows through the Strait of Hormuz. Europe is currently in its key summer storage injection period ahead of winter, but reduced LNG availability--particularly from Qatar--is making it harder to rebuild inventories, says Norman Liebke from Commerzbank. Lower inventories would leave Europe more exposed to supply shocks and winter demand spikes, keeping prices elevated at around 50 euros a megawatt-hour, according to the firm. Meanwhile, additional upside risks remain if hotter weather linked to El Nino boosts LNG demand across Asia this summer, increasing competition with Europe for available cargoes. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

data.title: '1873' Review: When the World Went on Sale
data.description:
By James Grant

In one single unforgettable day — Friday, May 9, 1873 — prices on the Vienna Stock Exchange plunged by 45%. All at once the world was changed.

"1873," by Liaquat Ahamed, author of "Lords of Finance: The Bankers Who Broke the World" (2009), is the story of the trans-Atlantic depression that followed the crash. It is the story, too, of a generation-long, befuddling decline in the prices of all kinds of things. In 1878, confronting the lowest prices for pig iron since colonial times, American ironmasters wondered if the smokestacks on their idled blast furnaces might serve a higher use as astronomical observatories.

Mr. Ahamed has a pleasant style, and he writes short, welcoming chapters. Pen portraits animate his cast of characters: bankers, especially the members of the opulent Rothschild clan; monetary cranks such as William Hope Harvey, the author of "Coin's Financial School" (1894), a bestselling paean to easy money; charlatans such as Bethel Henry Strousberg, a skyrocket of a German railroad baron; and a still-obscure Karl Marx, whose poorly reviewed first volume of "Das Kapital" (1867), according to Mr. Ahamed, didn't earn the cost of the cigars that its author "had puffed while writing it."

What Mr. Ahamed withholds are the strong, respectable arguments to embrace, not to shun, the boogeyman that Federal Reserve economists style "deflation" but that a layman might rather recognize as human progress. Prices fell in the final quarter of the 19th century in good part thanks to a transportation revolution that unified the world's commodity markets and to a wave of factory mechanization that collapsed production costs.

These days central bankers would meet such unwonted affordability with a gust of money-printing. It was the fear of falling prices in 2008, after all, that launched the Federal Reserve on its decadelong career in dollar-conjuring and interest-rate suppression.

Would an infusion of paper money not, at least, have softened the price-chopping transition to steam from sail, to rails from horses and to machinery from manpower? Mr. Ahamed is sure it would have helped, but the spirit of the times resisted it and the law of the land forbade it.

William Graham Sumner, in his "History of American Currency" (1874), had denounced the Supreme Court's legal-tender decisions of 1870-71, which affirmed the constitutional right of the government to issue paper money irredeemable in gold or silver. In so doing the Yale professor, Episcopalian clergyman and public intellectual drew a moral equivalency between the court's blessing of paper money (the kind that a creditor, by law, must accept in payment of a debt) and the 1857 Dred Scott case that gave legal sanction to human slavery. "It will not probably take a war to overthrow the principle of the legal-tender act," wrote Sumner, alluding to the Civil War, "but it may take a national bankruptcy."

So the decision to do nothing about the deflation was not a case of ignorance or vacillation, as Mr. Ahamed contends, but of alignment with long-established monetary norms. In 1874 Congress passed the so-called Inflation Bill, which would have expanded the supply of government-issued greenbacks. President Grant vetoed it. "It is a duty, and one of the highest duties, of Government," Grant explained, "to secure the citizen a medium of exchange of fixed, unvarying value. This implies a return to a specie basis" — the Civil War had taken the country off gold — "and no substitute for it can be devised."

To governments on both sides of the Atlantic, "specie" increasingly meant gold — not silver and gold together, but the single standard of gold alone. Overindebted railroad builders and heavily mortgaged farmers demanded a less rigorous, more forgiving silver unit, and debate raged between the adherents of "soft" and "hard" money — between silver and gold, inflation and solidity.

At a glance, the events of "1873" seem to adhere to standard cyclical form. First comes the bubble, in this case, 1867-72, then the bust, 1873-79, and at intervals thereafter. "The market had bullish orgies," an observer in Berlin reported before the bursting of the German bubble. "National prosperity rose to apparently unimaginable heights. A shower of gold rained down on the drunken city." By the close of 1873, German stock prices were down by 32% from their heights, Austria's by 70%. Out of 70 bubble-era Viennese banks, only eight survived the collapse. The U.S., not so roughly handled, suffered a 21% fall in share prices and 100 failed banks in comparison to 3,400 solvent survivors.

Was the prolonged, severe deflation of the final quarter of the 19th century so very fearful?

If a curse, it was a decidedly mixed one. Debtors suffered, to be sure, and it is to their side, and to that of Keynesians and paper-money enthusiasts yet unborn, that Mr. Ahamed rallies. But wage-earners — in textile manufacturing and railroading, to pick two examples — gained by the steady appreciation in the purchasing power of their hard-earned money. So, too, to be sure, did the Rothschild banking family as well as other, less-storied members of the international creditor class.

"The financial crisis of 1873 and the two-decade deflation that followed did more than inflict economic pain," the author argues. "As falling prices strained the social fabric, the buoyant optimism of boom years gave way to a pervasive mood of pessimism." Free trade yielded to protectionism and hope to resentment, as "politics took a darker turn."

The dark politics of money preoccupied Americans. It happens that legislation to remove silver from the list of Treasury-issued coins sailed through the House of Representatives in 1873. Perhaps few Congressmen bothered to read the bill, and all but 13 of 123 House members voted aye (it passed the Senate, 36-14). As it might have seemed, the issue was irrelevant, as gold continued to gain monetary market share over the cheaper, more plentiful alternative. Only later, in the vise of hard times, did easy-money conspiracy theorists fix on the demonetization of silver as the Crime of 1873.

Certainly, it was no crime, not even a conspiracy to commit a crime. Neither was it a violation of the Constitution as the firebrand George M. Weston, a Republican newspaper editor and silver propagandist, erroneously charged, even if Alexander Hamilton himself had favored a coequal role for gold and silver in American coinage.

Mr. Ahamed, an unapologetic silver man, goes so far in his advocacy of remedial government action as to suborn the long-departed author of "Lombard Street," published in 1873, to bear false witness. The "central recommendation" of Walter Bagehot's book, the author contends, is that a central bank must defeat a panic by "printing" money to promote recovery. Not true. What Bagehot actually said was that a central bank must face down a panic by lending freely at a high rate of interest to solvent banks against good collateral. Quantitative easing, or the creation of new paper money with which to buy government securities, seems never to have entered Bagehot's mind. Besides, gold and silver alone were money in Bagehot's opinion, and they were unprintable. Banknotes were merely promises to pay money, i.e., credit, not the monetary real McCoy.

Of all the bondholders, bloated and otherwise, in his story, Mr. Ahamed especially dotes on James Mayer de Rothschild (1792-1868), founder of the Banque Rothschild, financier of princes and railroads, patron of the arts, acquirer of the Château Lafite vineyard and builder of the Paris domicile that the radical poet Heinrich Heine called the "Versailles of the absolute monarchy of money." By the time of James's death, the consolidated Rothschild banking enterprise deployed $150 million of capital, "a third of the capital of the entire European banking system" at the time.

According to Mr. Ahamed, the subjugated silverite masses outnumbered the gold bug opposition, who were "almost exclusively drawn from the moneyed classes. The most fervent of them were the world's bond investors, several hundreds of thousands strong, and all their various bankers, whether based on Wall Street, at the Paris Bourse, or in the City of London." Yet when the 1896 presidential election pitted the soft-money populist, William Jennings Bryan, against William McKinley, the hard-money Republican, McKinley won the electoral majority, 271 to 176, and the popular vote, 51% to 46.7%, besides. So much for class conflict in gold-standard America.

  • Mr. Grant, the founder and editor of Grant's Interest Rate Observer, is the author of "Bagehot: The Greatest Victorian," among other books.

data.title: Music Licensing, Inc. (SONG) / Pro Music Rights Updates Standard Industrial Classification Code to 6189 - Asset-Backed Securities, Formalizing Its Structured Finance Identity
data.description:
SIC Reclassification Reinforces Company's Publicly Disclosed Strategy to Securitize Licensee Agreements, Copyright Infringement Claims, and Litigation Portfolio

Miami, Florida--(Newsfile Corp. - May 29, 2026) - Music Licensing, Inc. (OTCID: SONG) ("Music Licensing" or the "Company"), also known as Pro Music Rights, today announced that it has officially updated its Standard Industrial Classification ("SIC") code to 6189 – Asset-Backed Securities on the OTC Markets Group platform. The reclassification formally aligns the Company's market and regulatory designation with the structured finance strategy it disclosed publicly on May 10, 2026, and represents a natural progression of the Company's previously announced Strategic Transformation.

SPV Structure and Asset Scope

As disclosed in the Company's May 10, 2026 press release, Music Licensing is actively exploring and intends to form a Special Purpose Vehicle ("SPV") — structured in an internationally recognized jurisdiction — designed to securitize a diversified portfolio of revenue-generating and claim-based assets. The assets targeted for securitization include licensee agreements, copyright infringement claims, and the Company's broader litigation portfolio, which together represent a range of cash flow-generating and recoverable economic interests held by the Company and its wholly owned subsidiary, Pro Music Rights.

The SPV is intended to repackage these obligations and assets and issue them as Asset-Backed Securities ("ABS") and/or Structured Notes on a recurring, programmatic basis. The Company has disclosed its intention to target listings on international exchanges, as well as placements into the 144A and Regulation S markets, enabling distribution to qualified institutional buyers and non-U.S. persons under applicable exemptions from registration under the Securities Act of 1933, as amended.

The Company additionally notes that it is actively exploring whether it may utilize an existing Special Purpose Vehicle affiliated with one of its control parties, rather than forming an entirely new entity, which could expedite the timeline and reduce structural formation costs. The Company has stated that additional details regarding this determination will be provided to investors in a subsequent disclosure as discussions and due diligence progress.

Significance of the SIC 6189 Classification Update

The SIC 6189 designation — Asset-Backed Securities — is assigned to issuers whose primary operations involve the structuring, creation, and management of asset-backed securities instruments. This classification is self-reported and is recognized across institutional data providers, broker-dealer systems, and financial market infrastructure as the standard designation for ABS issuers. According to a company spokesperson, the update ensures that Music Licensing, Inc. is accurately represented across investor databases and market screening platforms consistent with the nature of the business it is actively building.

"This SIC code update reflects where the Company is operationally and strategically headed," a company spokesperson stated. "It is an accurate designation for a company that has publicly committed to a structured finance program built around asset-backed securities issuance, and we believe it will ensure proper categorization across institutional investor and data platforms."

Growing Institutional Market for Music ABS

The broader music royalty and music asset-backed securities market has expanded considerably in recent years, with institutional investors increasingly seeking exposure to royalty-backed cash flows as a low-correlation, long-duration asset class. Music royalty securitizations have attracted consistent institutional demand, and the sector continues to grow as rights holders and structured finance professionals develop increasingly sophisticated issuance frameworks. Music Licensing, Inc.'s disclosed strategy — spanning licensee agreement securitization, infringement claim monetization, and litigation portfolio structuring — represents a differentiated approach within this evolving asset class.

About Music Licensing, Inc. (OTCID: SONG) (ProMusicRights.com)

Music Licensing, Inc. (OTCID: SONG), also known as Pro Music Rights, is a diversified holding company and the fifth public performance rights organization (PRO) established in the United States. It is recognized under the federal registry of the United States government. The company licenses music to some of the most prominent platforms and businesses, including TikTok, iHeartMedia, Triller, Napster, 7Digital, Vevo, and many others.

Pro Music Rights holds an estimated 7.4% market share in the United States, representing a catalog of more than 2.5 million works by notable artists such as A$AP Rocky, Wiz Khalifa, Pharrell, Young Jeezy, Juelz Santana, Lil Yachty, MoneyBagg Yo, Larry June, Trae Pound, Sauce Walka, Trae Tha Truth, Sosamann, Soulja Boy, Lex Luger, Trauma Tone, Lud Foe, SlowBucks, Gunplay, OG Maco, Rich The Kid, Fat Trel, Young Scooter, Nipsey Hussle, Famous Dex, Boosie Badazz, Shy Glizzy, 2 Chainz, Migos, Gucci Mane, Young Dolph, Trinidad James, Chingy, Lil Gnar, 3OhBlack, Curren$y, Fall Out Boy, Money Man, Dej Loaf, Lil Uzi Vert, and many others, including works generated by artificial intelligence (AI).

Additionally, Music Licensing, Inc. (OTCID: SONG) holds royalty interests in Listerine "Mouthwash" Antiseptic and a vast portfolio of musical works by globally renowned artists, including The Weeknd, Justin Bieber, Kanye West, Elton John, Mike Posner, blackbear, Lil Nas X, Lil Yachty, DaBaby, Stunna 4 Vegas, Miley Cyrus, Lil Wayne, XXXTentacion, BlueFace, The Game, Jeremih, Ty Dolla $ign, Eric Bellinger, Ne-Yo, MoneyBagg Yo, Halsey, Desiigner, DaniLeigh, Rihanna, and many others.

Forward-Looking Statements:

This press release contains certain forward-looking statements within the meaning of Section 27A of the Securities Act of 1933, as amended and Section 21E of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, which are intended to be covered by the safe harbors created thereby. Investors are cautioned that, all forward-looking statements involve risks and uncertainties, including without limitation, the ability of Music Licensing, Inc. & Pro Music Rights, Inc. to accomplish its stated plan of business. Music Licensing, Inc. & Pro Music Rights, Inc. believes that the assumptions underlying the forward-looking statements contained herein are reasonable, any of the assumptions could be inaccurate, and therefore, there can be no assurance that the forward-looking statements included in this press release will prove to be accurate. In light of the significant uncertainties inherent in the forward-looking statements included herein, the inclusion of such information should not be regarded as a representation by Pro Music Rights, Inc., Music Licensing, Inc., or any other person.

Non-Legal Advice Disclosure:

This press release does not constitute legal advice, and readers are advised to seek legal counsel for any legal matters or questions related to the content herein.

Non-Investment Advice Disclosure:

This communication is intended solely for informational purposes and does not in any way imply or constitute a recommendation or solicitation for the purchase or sale of any securities, commodities, bonds, options, derivatives, or any other investment products. Any decisions related to investments should be made after thorough research and consultation with a qualified financial advisor or professional. We assume no liability for any actions taken or not taken based on the information provided in this communication

Contact: investors@ProMusicRights.com

SOURCE: Music Licensing, Inc.

data.title: Kalshi taps former FBI analyst Neff for surveillance unit
data.description:
By Anirban Sen

Kalshi has hired former FBI official Tyler Neff on its surveillance team, as part of a broader effort to ramp up safeguards on its exchange at a time when prediction markets are facing increased pressure from lawmakers to crack down on insider trading on their exchanges.

  • Neff joined Kalshi earlier in May, according to a post on LinkedIn. He reports to the company's head of enforcement and legal counsel, Robert DeNault, and will be part of a team responsible for all surveillance efforts, a spokeswoman for Kalshi said.

  • Neff previously served as an intel analyst on a white-collar crime squad at the New York field office of the FBI for seven years. His role at the law enforcement agency was followed by stints at the New York Stock Exchange, Wedbush Securities, and Canaccord Genuity.

  • Kalshi has ramped up hiring on its surveillance unit this year, tapping a handful of other executives from Wall Street financial institutions like Morgan Stanley and Nasdaq.

  • The latest moves come as top prediction markets have witnessed a surge in suspicious trades in recent months. Since the start of this year, Kalshi has probed and flagged more than 400 suspicious trades, more than twice the number of trades that the platform investigated all of last year, Reuters reported earlier in May.

  • Earlier this week, the U.S. Justice Department charged a Google software engineer with using insider information to rig bets tied to Google's most-searched list on Polymarket.

data.title: Medline Says Medline Canada Signs Prime Vendor Agreement With Mohawk Medbuy
data.description:

data.title: RBC Steps Up AI Ambitions With Vancouver Hub Expansion
data.description:
By Adriano Marchese

Royal Bank of Canada is expanding its Vancouver, British Columbia, tech hub as it accelerates investments in artificial intelligence and builds out the infrastructure needed for enterprise-wide deployment.

The Canadian financial institution said Friday that it plans to grow its presence in the province by expanding it banking and innovation hub which includes the Vancouver Borealis AI Hub.

The move will integrate AI capabilities across cloud, cyber, digital and analytics as RBC works toward generating 700 million Canadian dollars ($507.8 million) to C$1 billion in AI-driven enterprise value.

The bank plans to scale up its operations at the Vancouver hub, expanding its staffing footprint to 600 by the end of the year, from 350 currently and ultimately, support about 1,000 jobs across the province by 2029.

RBC also intends for the hub to serve key client groups within its commercial banking business, it said.

Write to Adriano Marchese at adriano.marchese@wsj.com
